title: Ants Climbing a Tree

overview.description: A Sichuan dish of mung bean glass noodles cooked with minced pork, fermented chilli bean paste and aromatic seasonings.

ingredients: 1½ tbsp doubanjiang (Sichuan fermented chilli bean paste)
method: Soak the mung bean glass noodles according to the packet instructions until softened but not completely tender.
Heat the oil in a wok or large frying pan over medium-high heat.
Stir-fry until the pork is browned and any excess moisture has cooked away.
Stir-fry for 1–2 minutes until the oil becomes aromatic and the paste is well incorporated.
Add the Shaoxing wine, light soy sauce, dark soy sauce and sugar.
Add the chicken stock and bring to a simmer.
Cook gently until the noodles are tender and have absorbed much of the seasoned stock.
Add the ground Sichuan pepper and sesame oil.
Garnish with spring onions and coriander if using.

learning.did-you-know: The name Ants Climbing a Tree refers to the small pieces of minced pork clinging to the long glass noodles, which are imagined as ants climbing branches.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How fermented chilli bean paste creates depth and savouriness, and how glass noodles absorb the flavours of a strongly seasoned broth.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Sichuan noodle dish that provides a final textural contrast to the collection, using mung bean glass noodles that absorb a deeply savoury and spicy sauce.
chapman-tips: Fen si, or mung bean glass noodles, are traditionally used for this dish.
Don't over-soak the noodles before cooking; they should finish cooking in the seasoned stock.
Doubanjiang varies considerably in saltiness and heat, so adjust the quantity to taste.
The minced pork should be browned well before the liquid is added.
The noodles should absorb most of the stock rather than sit in a large amount of liquid.
Sichuan pepper provides a distinctive numbing sensation as well as citrus-like aroma.
For a milder family version, reduce the doubanjiang and serve extra chilli oil separately.
The dish is best served immediately while the noodles are glossy and the sauce is still rich.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh. The glass noodles continue absorbing liquid as they stand and can become soft when reheated.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently with a splash of water or stock. The noodles may need loosening as they absorb more liquid during storage.
